perturbation.original_text: a) During the Term, Supplier shall Manufacture the Product and perform all Services at its facilities located at Bubendorf, Switzerland and at Neuland, Switzerland (such facilities, the "Facilities" and each, a "Facility"). Supplier will supply to Customer or Customer's designee, the Product, Manufactured in accordance with the accepted Purchase Order placed by Customer, Master Batch Record, the Product Specifications, the Quality Agreement and cGMP Requirements and, subject to Section 3.1(b), in such quantities as ordered by Customer in Purchase Orders submitted pursuant to Section 6.1 and accepted pursuant to Section 6.2.
perturbation.changed_text: a) During the Term, Supplier may Manufacture the Product and perform all Services at facilities it deems suitable. Supplier will supply to Customer or Customer's designee, the Product, Manufactured in accordance with the accepted Purchase Order placed by Customer, internal procedures, and standards it considers appropriate and, subject to Section 3.1(b), in such quantities as ordered by Customer in Purchase Orders submitted pursuant to Section 6.1 and accepted pursuant to Section 6.2.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing explicit references to 'cGMP Requirements', 'Master Batch Record', the 'Quality Agreement' and specific facilities with generalized terms ('internal procedures', 'standards it considers appropriate' and 'facilities it deems suitable'), the contract becomes ambiguous and potentially contradicts established manufacturing and quality control regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR § 211.5 - Current good manufacturing practice for finished pharmaceuticals
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 21 CFR § 211.5, which mandates adherence to current good manufacturing practices (CGMP) in the production of pharmaceuticals. By removing the explicit requirement to follow cGMP, the modified text allows for manufacturing practices that may not meet federal standards, potentially leading to the production of unsafe or ineffective drugs. Internal procedures may not comply with regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.

perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly requires adherence to 'cGMP Requirements', 'Master Batch Record', and the 'Quality Agreement', referencing specific facilities for manufacturing. The changed text replaces these with more general terms like 'internal procedures' and 'facilities it deems suitable'. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts 21 CFR § 211.5, which mandates adherence to current good manufacturing practices (CGMP) in the production of pharmaceuticals. By removing the explicit requirement to follow cGMP, the modified text allows for manufacturing practices that may not meet federal standards. Because the edited contract removes the requirement to follow GMP, a clear legal standard, it creates a contradiction. Although both scraped snippets do not explicitly state this, the law explanation does, and it's clear based on the provided definitions that this falls under a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: i) Supplier does not, at any time from and after the Effective Date, retain or use the services of (i) any person debarred under 21 U.S.C. § 335a or (ii) any person who has been convicted of a crime as defined under the FD&C Act, in each case in any capacity associated with or related to the Manufacture or supply of Products or any service rendered to Customer under this Agreement or the Quality Agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: i) Supplier shall endeavor to avoid using the services of (i) any person previously warned by the FDA or (ii) any person with a criminal record, in any capacity associated with the Manufacture or supply of Products or any service rendered to Customer under this Agreement or the Quality Agreement;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'debarred under 21 U.S.C. § 335a' to 'previously warned by the FDA' and 'convicted of a crime as defined under the FD&C Act' to 'person with a criminal record' weakens the clause significantly. Debarment under 21 U.S.C. § 335a has a specific legal meaning and consequences, and avoiding 'endeavor to avoid' weakens what was a requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S.C. § 335a - Debarment, temporary denial of approval, and suspension
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text explicitly prohibits using individuals debarred under 21 U.S.C. § 335a, a legally defined term with specific consequences. The modified text weakens this by using 'endeavor to avoid' and replacing 'debarred' with 'previously warned,' which carries no legal weight. This weakens the supplier's obligation and potentially violates the spirit of regulations aimed at preventing disqualified individuals from participating in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

Debarment, temporary denial of approval, and suspension (a) Mandatory debarment; certain drug applications (1) Corporations, partnerships, and associations If the Secretary finds that a person other than an individual has been convicted, after May 13, 1992, of a felony under Federal law for conduct relating to the deve
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly prohibits the use of individuals debarred under 21 U.S.C. § 335a, which carries a specific legal meaning as evidenced by the scraped snippet which is titled 'Debarment, temporary denial of approval, and suspension'. The changed text weakens this prohibition by using the phrase 'endeavor to avoid' and by replacing the term 'debarred' with 'previously warned', which does not have the same legal weight. By weakening the requirement, and using a term without legal meaning, the changed text creates a contradiction by undermining the supplier's original obligation and potentially violating regulations aimed at preventing disqualified individuals from participating in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

perturbation.original_text: f) all Product delivered to Customer under this Agreement will have been Manufactured, stored and shipped in a competent fashion in accordance with the Master Batch Record, the Product Specifications, this Agreement, the Quality Agreement, Applicable Law and cGMP Requirements by qualified personnel and, to Supplier's knowledge, will be free from defects;
perturbation.changed_text: f) all Product delivered to Customer under this Agreement will have been Manufactured, stored and shipped in a generally acceptable fashion, following internal documentation, by personnel deemed competent by Supplier's management and, to Supplier's understanding, will be reasonably free from defects;
perturbation.explanation: By removing explicit references to 'Master Batch Record', 'Product Specifications', 'Quality Agreement', 'Applicable Law', 'cGMP Requirements' and 'qualified personnel' and replacing them with vague terms ('generally acceptable fashion', 'internal documentation', 'personnel deemed competent by Supplier's management', 'reasonably free from defects'), the contract introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with established manufacturing and quality control regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR § 211.22 - Responsibilities of quality control unit
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts 21 CFR § 211.22, which outlines the responsibilities of the quality control unit in pharmaceutical manufacturing. The original text ensures compliance with established quality standards and regulatory requirements. The modified text replaces these with subjective assessments and internal documentation, undermining the independent oversight and quality assurance mandated by the regulation. 'Internal documentation' is not legally adequate without specific regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces specific legal and regulatory requirements with vague terms, undermining quality control standards. The Law Explanation states this directly contradicts 21 CFR § 211.22, which outlines quality control responsibilities. The original contract text requires compliance with standards such as 'Master Batch Record' and 'cGMP Requirements,' while the changed text uses 'generally acceptable fashion' and 'internal documentation'. This deviation from legally recognized definitions creates a contradiction.
